notes: DOCUMENTED TARGET — NOT YET RECORDED (driver_gap:permission-approval). The agent invokes a tool that trips a permission prompt; the session enters `waiting` and stays there until the user resolves it (approve/deny). User-observable signal (matching the claudecode sibling): ready → working → waiting, final state waiting while the prompt is open, then a clean waiting → working → ready resolution once approved. SESSION IDENTITY (opencode-specific): opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter (agent.go) — session state lives in the SQLite store and the daemon observes a UUID-keyed `ses_…` session; the driver chains turns with `--session <id>`. session_birth is anchored to recording start and left UNPINNED so a transient proc-<PID> presession cannot steal identity and cascade same_session_as failures; turn_start (matching the FIRST working in the recording, necessarily the rollout session — the presession never enters working) establishes the identity every later phase pins to via same_session_as. WHY NOT RECORDABLE TODAY (driver_gap:permission-approval): opencode's permission gate is TUI-only. The rig drives opencode via headless `opencode run`, which AUTO-EXECUTES tools with NO permission prompt — PROVEN by the sibling auto-executed-tool-call recording, where gemma's `write` tool ran automatically inside the working span with zero waiting/approval state. So the `waiting` episode this spec asserts cannot arise on the headless driver. Even if it did, drive-opencode-interactive.sh implements ONLY send / wait_turn (no-op) / sleep — there is NO approve/deny (or keys) step to RESOLVE a prompt, so the waiting → working/ready resolution half is unreachable. Eliciting AND resolving a permission gate requires a live-REPL opencode driver. This spec is retained as the recordable target once the driver gains such a primitive (a developer task); see assessment.json (driver_capability: gap:permission-approval). NOTE the daemon side is unproven: opencode's store-reading parser/watcher have no permission/waiting branch, so whether a permission waiting episode is observable from the SQLite store cannot be confirmed until a live-REPL driver elicits one (a likely observability_correction if/when recorded). HOW runs differ (once recorded): exact tool wording, the ses_… UUID/PID, per-turn token counts, and sub-second timing all vary. Stable structurally: ready→working→waiting on ONE ses_… session, a clean waiting→working→ready resolution on the same session after approval, and a single process_exited.

text: A session appears in ready within 2s of agent launch (the ses_… store session)
The tool-inducing prompt drives the ses_… session ready → working (binds the rollout session; a transient presession never enters working)
PID bound to the rollout ses_… session
The tool invocation trips the permission prompt; the SAME ses_… session goes working → waiting and stays there while the prompt is open (no auto-resolve)
The user approves the prompt; the SAME ses_… session resumes waiting → working as the tool runs and the turn continues
The approved turn completes back to ready on the same ses_… session (ready→working→waiting→working→ready arc)
Agent torn down once after the turn → a single process_exited on the rollout session; the live state immediately before teardown is ready
